speaker: interviewer
text: Manya, you said you ran into the forest after staying with this Ukrainian woman. Can you tell me what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was running out, there was a tremendous snowstorm. And I was terribly hungry. And I-- I-- I was terribly cold. So I decided-- I saw the-- I saw the next house. I knocked on the door. They were all sitting and eating their breakfast. And I asked them if they could give me something to eat. They started screaming at me and yelling, you Jewess. And you have a nerve to do that. And-- and-- and because of you, that-- that's all the trouble in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to them, I'll tell you. I said-- this is exactly what I said-- it-- you see all these things that's happening to us? You are not exempt. It's going to happen to you, just like to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I found out later, about 10 minutes later, the Germans came, and they killed them all around the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided, since I had a command of the Russian language so good, I'm going to change my story. I'm not going to pose as a Jew. I'm going to say that I'm a Russian teacher, and that I escaped from that village. And as I was walking the road, the villagers were still coming in. They were asking me, what's new? What's going on in Berezniaki? So I said, well, Germans came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take long. I turned around. And the village was on fire. They-- they burned the village. They put it on fire from all four sides. And they took the people. They tricked them. They took them into church. And they offered them some candy and some chocolate. And then they took them out, and they shot them. They-- they started shooting whoever they could. None of them escaped. They shot-- they killed the entire village. I-- I was very cold, as I said, and hungry. And so I-- I-- I walked up to another house. And I said to them-- and the-- the reason I decided to change my story, because I already was a few kilometers from the village. And I thought that nobody heard about me. So I thought, maybe they'll think I'm telling the truth. And I said, I'm a Russian teacher. And-- and I ran away. The Germans are here. They were all sitting, already prepared of running, because they knew what was going on. She said, I'm sorry. We cannot help you. Here is a piece of bread and go. So I wen. And I walked another kilometer. And I came another-- across another house. And the guy was sitting in front. And I come up. And I started telling him this story. And he said to me, my dear, you don't have to tell me who you are. I know exactly who you are. Don't come up with stories. We know what's happening. And we know what happened to the Jews. And now it's happening to us. First of all, come, let me take you into the house. And I'll feed you. He gave me breakfast. And I said to him-- and he said, you know, not far from here, there are Jews hiding. And I'm going to take you there. I said, oh, this is-- this is my wish. I know I'm not going to live. But I want to die among my own. And after I ate, his boys took me. And they-- we were walking. I had a pair of strong boots. The snow was up to my knees. And he took us to a-- a-- a swampy area, where it was barren. There were no woods. He said, cross it. And this is where you'll find your Jews. As I crossed it, I came across a group of farmers. They were tremendously organized. They had all their horses and their wagons. And they knew the areas. They knew every nook and cranny. It was swampy. And it was very dangerous to walk it. Don't forget, it was March already. And it started thawing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they knew where to get. And the Germans were literally combing these woods. But they organized themselves. And they had four people, in each direction, watching of where the Germans are going, so they could run in a different way, in a different area.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came up. And I told them. And they said, don't worry. We know who you are. You stay with us. And we're going to give you some food, whatever they had. They had some hard cheeses and hard bread. And the first day, they were nice to me. But the next day, they were horrible. They wanted to kill me. They were beating me up. They were calling me spy. And I said to them, look, this is the situation. If you are-- if you want to kill me, you kill me right here. But I don't know. I have a hunch that you're going to be safe here. I'm staying with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with them for-- they could not have a fire, because, you know, the-- the fire is seen and, also, the smoke can be felt. They didn't have a fire, like, for 10 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sleeping by the wagon. I woke up. And I had about a foot of snow on top of me. And I froze. My feet were frozen at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after 10 days, the shooting stopped. And so they decided to go to their village to see what's happening. They came, and they found two women, who were wounded. One was near death, and the other one was not bad. So they made a proposition to me. They said, since you have no place to go, why don't we put you in a little bunker? And you're going to take care of the women. And we're going to supply you with food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said fine. So they did. They put me in the little bunker. And the next day, they brought a little bit of soup. But the following night, the woman-- one woman died. So they came, and they buried her. And that was the end of their visit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remained with the other woman. As I was lying there, she-- she was telling me, you know, somebody will come to-- to-- to look for me, my nephew. And if he'll come, you'll go with me. And I'll treat you like my daughter. You'll help me out in-- in my chores around the house. And everything will be fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said OK. And we were lying there. While I was around their wagons, I managed to steal a little bit of flour, and I put it in my pocket. And I had some matches. And I had some salt. And the-- the nephew finally came. And he got her. And as soon as he came, she says, I don't want that dirty Jew with me. Come on. Let's go. And he left me, right there, in the-- in that-- that little hut. I was lying. I-- I took a little bit of the flour and a little bit of water, and I mixed it. I-- I stayed for a couple days. But I was terribly hungry. So all of a sudden, a farmer passed by.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, oh, my God, how are you surviving here alone? He said, you know, not far from here, there are burned out houses. And there are the Jewish people that are live there-- that are roaming in the forest. They come out to-- to get some potatoes and vegetable in the burned out houses, in the cellars. Why don't you go-- try to go out to that burned out house? And you will meet them there. So I did. I crawled out on my four-- it took me a half a day to get there. And I came, and I waited, and I waited. When it started getting dark, I saw they are not coming out. So I grabbed-- there was a-- a-- a rooster and a chicken that were drowned. I took some potatoes, on my shoulders, and I went back to my bunker. And I came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a broken pot. And I cooked some chicken soup. And I sat there. And I ate. And I thought, whatever it's going to be. The wolves were screaming, you know, at night. This, I didn't worry. But when it started thundering and storming, I thought, now I'm going to get killed. Anyway, to make the long story short, he came again, the farmer. He sort of felt terribly sorry for me. And he said, these son of a guns, I'm going to get them. He came and, by force, he forced them to come and get me. So there were seven of them, seven males. There was one father and two sons, and another father with two sons, that also escaped from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed-- I-- they took me into their bunker, and we stayed. And how did we? It started getting already spring. [SIGHS] And there were an awful lot of cows and horses lying dead. So we used to go and cut pieces of meat and get some potatoes from the cellars, of the burned out houses, and bake them. And then-- then, when it started getting warmer, and the farmers started rebuilding their homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started working the fields. So they used to hire us for our-- for a day's-- day food. We used to go. And they'd work the field. It was horrible work, very hard, but for the food. And then, the local-- the-- the area of the partisans started getting to rebuild. Because, you see, the Germans were already afraid to come to this area. The-- the partisans did not disappear. That big group, of the 5,000 people, went south. They went into the Carpathian mountains. And this is where my sister, my brother, and my father went, without knowing what happened. They found out later. And I found it out through my cousin, who survived and lived in Israel, that somebody told them that I was left. And I was-- and they-- they cried after me, because they were sure that I am dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with-- that was in the spring. And we were doing. And-- and-- and as the-- what we contract, that living-dead bunker, is-- is-- for-- for a human person, not to believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got scabs. We were full of lice. We were full of the-- the-- the wild rats in the forest were running under our heads. It was so horrible to live with the scabs, because we were itching. That when we were scratching, it was burning like fire. And as the local partisans started building up, there was a Jewish doctor among them. He came out. And he made-- he prepared a remedy from-- for us. And we smeared ourselves. And then we, sort of, like, disinfected our clothes on top of the fire. And we started getting a little bit better. As the local partisans started rebuilding itself, they needed some more people. And they came. And they-- they asked me, since they knew that I am a former partisan. I was with that big group. And I had experience of how to deal with people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they asked me if I would like to rejoin the local partisans. And they even told me that, not far from there is a Jewish partisan group composed of 120 Jews that escaped one ghetto. And I said, oh, yes. This will be the dream of my life. So I marched with them at night. I helped them carry their equipment. And we reached that Jewish group. And I-- I stayed with the Jewish group, but there was an awful lot of antisemitism against us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give us any weapons. We had 10 rifles. Eight of them were broken. And with these rifles, we managed only to get food for ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were-- there were a couple of incidents, that I must tell you, that are important to this story. I started staying with one girl, in that partisan group, who was from a little town of 1,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way they killed them is they took them out, outside of town. And they were holding each Jew by the hair, and they were shooting in the back of their head. Towards night-- she was the last one. She had a little girl, five years old. She-- she was standing. And he was going to shoot her. But his hand, the Ukrainian's hand, was chasing from doing so much shooting that her-- the-- the bullet didn't penetrate her head, but it grazed her hairline. And the-- in front of her, they shot her little girl and threw her into the grave. And she fell into the grave. And since it was getting dark, and they were done, they shot all the people. They just went with a machine gun and put some more bullets. But luckily enough, she didn't catch one. And they all left. when she didn't hear any voices, she crawled out of that grave. And she met-- she saw another girl, who was also shot through the arm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So both of them started running. And all of a sudden, they noticed that they are naked. So they went back, and they took some clothes from the pile of the clothes that was lying near the graves. And they ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they ran into two different directions. She ran into a house, whose son was one of the policeman that was killing the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came home, he said to her, you know, I know you from that little town. You're a pretty girl. I'm going to save you. And when the war will be over, I'm going to marry you. Can you imagine that nerve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, her brother and two cousins found out about her. And at night-- and they-- they came at night, and they got her out of there. I also was with another girl, who escaped with her husband. And she was in that group. And she left her little boy with her mother. And he-- and he-- he was killed in the ghetto, with the-- with the grandparents. She unfortunately became pregnant in the forest. And she-- she-- she gave birth to a little girl. But while she was pregnant, her husband wanted to please her, so he went to the farmers. And he asked for a little bit of honey, because, you know, she was-- she was undernourished, she was pregnant. At that time, there was an order, from the top Russians, that they should not-- they do-- that the partisans should stop looting the farmers and gain them on our side. And somebody noticed him, that he was taking some honey from the farmer. And they told on him. And they court-martialed him. And they killed him. They shot him to death. And she gave birth to the little baby six months after he died. And I was assisting with the birth. And when the baby was six months old, we had to run. Because the Germans were chasing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she had to give up the baby-- and the baby started crying. So they came in. They said to her, it's either the-- a group of 120 people or your daughter is going to be killed. So she had no choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave up her girl. So the girl-- they took the girl. And they took her to a farmer. And they said to the farmer, you better take good care of that girl. She is one of the partisans. And if you're not going to take care of her, we're going to kill her. Anyway, that girl stayed with that farmer. And when the Germans came in, by the time the mother wanted to run and get the-- get the girl, they-- the Germans came in, and they took pity on the little infant. And they didn't touch that woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we were-- so I stayed with that the group from 1943 until, in '44, we were liberated by the army. This-- this group, the local groups, were doing the same kind of work as the other big group of Kovpak I was telling you. And I served as a nurse. They were-- What was the name of that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaganovich. Kaganovich was the brother-in-law of Stalin. And he was Jewish. And since they were all Jews, so they decided to name that group after Kaganovich. Now, they did not supply us, because they were so antisemitic. And they were so against Jews, in spite of the fact that we were called partisans, they would not supply us with any ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Jew-- the Jewish guys that were with us, in that group, they came on a very noble idea. They took a tree. And they chopped it up into a triangle, a long triangle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at night, they took it on their shoulders, and they brought it to the railways. And they-- to the railways, and they attached it. And at night, when the-- the train was coming, the locomotive fell off, and-- and the train couldn't go any further.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- this news spread among the partisans. And they thought that it was a tremendous, heroic thing to do. So they came, and they gave us dynamite, and they gave us more rifles. And they told us, now, you can act as a-- as a regular partisan group. So we picked a-- a-- a mission to go and damage two kilometers of railway. I served as a nurse. And they gave each soldier-- they gave each partisan, they gave a piece of dynamite, which was like a piece of soap, a bar of soap, with a wick inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took matches. And we had a-- a guard that what lived in the area, and he knew the area very well. So at night, he took us. And we took out-- we went out to like about 15 kilometers to the railway. It was a very, very damp night. And the Germans were working on the railways. They were constantly fixing it. So they had-- and they had barracks that they lived, not far. So they had bonfires, that they were warming up. And as the guys were dispersed on the railways, and they were going to light the wicks and put the dynamite under the rails, the-- the matches wouldn't work. They were damp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they ran to the coals, and they grabbed the coals. And they were lighting the wick. This created a little bit of a commotion. And the Germans woke up, and they started chasing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sitting, with a guard, in one corner. And he became very-- I-- I was told that this is my punkt, my point, where they will come, and they were-- we will reunite and run back. But he became panicky. And he said, I'm not staying to get killed. He started running. And I ran after him. And Germans were chasing me. And I could see them, practically. They were pointing the gun. And they were shooting. And the bullets were flying by my side. And I came to a-- a ditch. And a-- a tree was lying across the ditch. I crossed over. And it-- they-- and they were yelling, halt! But they wouldn't cross the ditch. And this is how all of us crossed it. We had one casualty. One of us was killed. And we managed to damage two kilometers of railways. There was-- did an awful lot of damage to the trains. It held up the trains that were delivering supplies to the front lines. And this kind of work, we're doing-- we were doing all the time until-- between-- between '43-- in-- in '43, September, till about July of '44. In '44, the-- the-- the front lines came already close. The Germans started to get defeated. They were starting to being defeated. And we were liberated around that city of Pinsk. And we were brought in, as partisans, into the city. And the majority of young boys were-- were mobilized into the army. And I was given a job to-- to work as a secretary for the 'uncovered dead.' That's for the Secret Service. I worked at night. I worked there till 1 o'clock. And this is how I stayed in Pinsk, up until in 1944. In '45, I went back to take a look at my city, where I was born. There was nothing there. It was-- there was blood and graves. I saw there was nothing for me to do, so I went back to Pinsk. And from Pinsk, we got papers. And we were-- we went deeper into Poland, toward the German border, because we heard that there are-- the-- all the homeless and the displaced persons were being organized and put into DP camps. And this is how we smuggled the border of Poland and Germany. We got into the DP camp in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you moved to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moved to Berlin. That's where I met my husband, and I-- my older son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went from Pinsk to Berlin, how did you travel? By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, everything was by train, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and this was already after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- in 1945, in May, the war was ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We can only do that after the war ended. When the war ended-- this-- there were thousands of us. We were put into three camps in Berlin. One camp was 3,000 people. The other camp was 2,000 people. And I stayed in a little camp of 200 people. They fed us, and they gave us clothes. And we started doing a little business on our own. I met my husband, and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: This would be? Your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herman Auster, A-U-S-T-E-R. My oldest son, Barry, was born in 1949. And the-- the camps were dispersed in 1948. They took them all into West-- West Germany, put them into other camps on the way to either America or, at that time, Palestine. They were going illegally. We stayed private. We were-- we rented an apartment. And we stayed in Berlin. And my husband was doing a little bit of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was he involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was black market-- black market business, yes. And that's how we survived. And in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1950, of-- in-- in December, we immigrated to America. My son was 15 months old. We came to America. We settled in Detroit. I had another son, Steven. And I became a Hebrew teacher. I taught in Shaarey Zedek for 25 years. My first husband passed away in 1965. And both of my boys became doctors. One is a dermatologist, here, in Detroit. And my other one is a child psychiatrist in Boston. I have four granddaughters, 17, 14, 11, and 7.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to ask you if there's anything else that you wanted to add to your story? Any things that you remember that-- that we may have forgotten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you something. This, what, took an hour and a half? It's-- I-- I barely scratched the surface of what I was telling. I mean, it's so many things that happened, while-- human stories and unhuman stories. By the way, the-- the person, the farmer, that saved us, right after we escaped the ghetto, I-- I think of him an-- a-- a long-- an-- an awful many times, because he was a very, very noble human being. And for-- he paid. He and his wife and his son and daughter-in-law and the children paid with his life. His Ukrainian brethren came and-- and killed them and burned down his house for helping Jews. There's the-- that's the inhuman part. The human part, it was of-- one person that helped out Jews. And the reason I am doing it-- this is my second tape. I did one tape in 1983. I feel that each and every one of us survivors has a tremendous mission to accomplish of telling the story of our survival. Because in view of the fact of what's going on, with the people that are coming out, the reformists, saying that it's a hoax, it's not happen, we are the witnesses. We lived through it. And we know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talk to my friends, survivors. And I urge them. Many of them cannot talk about it. And to tell you the truth, I can see their point of-- What they went through, in concentration camps, does not compare, no way, to-- to my life. Even though of I-- what I went through, having hunger and having-- and-- and being in cold and-- and-- and-- and being so sick, I-- I was running from death. I wasn't facing death. And I felt that we-- we still contributed something by fighting the Germans and doing some damage to them. And now-- well, that's the end of this story. Many of us are not among the living anymore. And [CRYING] this story has to be told, definitely. [CRYING] [SOBBING] It's a-- it's a-- it's an obligation. I-- I get into discussions, very heated discussions, urging them, that they must come out and tell this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Manya, we thank you so much for telling your story. It's been a privilege to talk with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let us go to your pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Yes. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Can you tell me the first and last name of the person in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is Sonia Barman, my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, as-- as--

speaker: interviewer
text: About.

speaker: interviewee
text: --as you can-- the-- this photo was taken when she was very young, before she got married. But there's a very interesting story attached to it. As I told you, I-- when we escaped, we didn't have no pictures. I salvaged this picture-- picture from my uncle. You see, my father was an invalid, when he came from-- Before the First World War, he had a-- a girlfriend, who was-- whom he was supposed to marry. But when he came back, and he lost his four fingers, she didn't want him. And she went to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went, and he found my mother. And he married her. And when he married her, he sent a picture to this girlfriend. And she lived in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle, who lived in Washington, when I survived and I came to America, he said, you know, I have a picture of your mother. And this is how I got this picture. I also have a picture of my-- of my uncle and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll show that next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what city this was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in David Grudek, two names, D-A-V-I-D G-R-U-D-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that would be in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. That's in Poland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
